### Baptismal formula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Baptismal formula")]

Main article: [Trinitarian formula](/wiki/Trinitarian_formula "Trinitarian formula")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Piero%2C_battesimo_di_cristo_04.jpg/250px-Piero%2C_battesimo_di_cristo_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piero,_battesimo_di_cristo_04.jpg)

*[The Baptism of Christ](/wiki/The_Baptism_of_Christ_(Piero_della_Francesca) "The Baptism of Christ (Piero della Francesca)")*, by [Piero della Francesca](/wiki/Piero_della_Francesca "Piero della Francesca"), 15th century

Baptism is generally conferred with the [Trinitarian formula](/wiki/Trinitarian_formula "Trinitarian formula"), "in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit".[[118]](#cite_note-120) Trinitarians identify this name with the Christian faith into which baptism is an initiation, as seen, for example, in the statement of [Basil the Great](/wiki/Basil_the_Great "Basil the Great") (330–379): "We are bound to be baptized in the terms we have received, and to profess faith in the terms in which we have been baptized." The [First Council of Constantinople](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople") (381) also says, "This is the Faith of our baptism that teaches us to believe in the Name of the Father, of the Son and of the Holy Spirit. According to this Faith there is one Godhead, Power, and Being of the Father, of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit."[[119]](#cite_note-121) This may be taken to indicate that baptism was associated with this formula from the earliest decades of the Church's existence. Other Trinitarian formulas found in the New Testament include 2 Corinthians 13:14, 1 Corinthians 12:4–6, Ephesians 4:4–6, 1 Peter 1:2, and Revelation 1:4–5.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJanuariy201399-15)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFee200252-34)

[Oneness Pentecostals](/wiki/Oneness_Pentecostals "Oneness Pentecostals") demur from the Trinitarian view of baptism and emphasize baptism "in the name of Jesus Christ" only, what they hold to be the original apostolic formula.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVondey201278-122) For this reason, they often focus on the baptisms in Acts. Those who place great emphasis on the baptisms in Acts often likewise question the authenticity of Matthew 28:19 in its present form.[[121]](#cite_note-123)[[122]](#cite_note-124) Most scholars of New Testament [textual criticism](/wiki/Textual_criticism "Textual criticism") accept the authenticity of the passage since there are no variant manuscripts regarding the formula,[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerguson2009134–135-56) and the extant form of the passage is attested in the [Didache](/wiki/Didache "Didache")[[123]](#cite_note-patristics-125) and other [patristic](/wiki/Patristic "Patristic") works of the 1st and 2nd centuries: [Ignatius](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch"),[[124]](#cite_note-patristics1-126) [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian"),[[125]](#cite_note-patristics2-127) [Hippolytus](/wiki/Hippolytus_(writer) "Hippolytus (writer)"),[[126]](#cite_note-patristics3-128) [Cyprian](/wiki/Cyprian "Cyprian"),[[127]](#cite_note-patristics4-129) and [Gregory Thaumaturgus](/wiki/Gregory_Thaumaturgus "Gregory Thaumaturgus").[[128]](#cite_note-patristics5-130)

Commenting on Matthew 28:19, [Gerhard Kittel](/wiki/Gerhard_Kittel "Gerhard Kittel") states:

> This threefold relation [of Father, Son and Spirit] soon found fixed expression in the triadic formulae in 2 Corinthians 13:14[[129]](#cite_note-131) and in 1 Corinthians 12:4–6.[[130]](#cite_note-132) The form is first found in the baptismal formula in Matthew 28:19 Did., 7. 1 and 3. ... [I]t is self-evident that Father, Son and Spirit are here linked in an indissoluble threefold relationship.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

### Economic and immanent Trinity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economic and immanent Trinity")]

The term "immanent Trinity" focuses on who God is; the term "economic Trinity" focuses on what God does. According to the [Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church"):

> The Fathers of the Church distinguish between theology (*theologia*) and economy (*oikonomia*). "Theology" refers to the mystery of God's inmost life within the Blessed Trinity and "economy" to all the works by which God reveals himself and communicates his life. Through the *oikonomia* the *theologia* is revealed to us; but conversely, the *theologia* illuminates the whole *oikonomia*. God's works reveal who he is in himself; the mystery of his inmost being enlightens our understanding of all his works. So it is, analogously, among human persons. A person discloses himself in his actions, and the better we know a person, the better we understand his actions.[[132]](#cite_note-134)

> The whole divine economy is the common work of the three divine persons. For as the Trinity has only one and the same natures so too does it have only one and the same operation: "The Father, the Son and the Holy Spirit are not three principles of creation but one principle." However, each divine person performs the common work according to his unique personal property. Thus the Church confesses, following the New Testament, "one God and Father from whom all things are, and one Lord Jesus Christ, through whom all things are, and one Holy Spirit in whom all things are". It is above all the divine missions of the Son's Incarnation and the gift of the Holy Spirit that show forth the properties of the divine persons.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

The ancient [Nicene theologians](/wiki/Nicene_Christianity "Nicene Christianity") argued that everything the Trinity does is done by Father, Son, and Spirit working in unity with one will. The three persons of the Trinity always work inseparably, for their work is always the work of the one God. The Son's will cannot be different from the Father's because it is the Father's. They have but one will as they have but one being. Otherwise, they would not be one God. On this point [St. Basil](/wiki/St._Basil "St. Basil") said:

> When then He says, "I have not spoken of myself", and again, "As the Father said unto me, so I speak", and "The word which ye hear is not mine, but [the Father's] which sent me", and in another place, "As the Father gave me commandment, even so I do", it is not because He lacks deliberate purpose or power of initiation, nor yet because He has to wait for the preconcerted key-note, that he employs language of this kind. His object is to make it plain that His own will is connected in indissoluble union with the Father. Do not then let us understand by what is called a "commandment" a peremptory mandate delivered by organs of speech, and giving orders to the Son, as to a subordinate, concerning what He ought to do. Let us rather, in a sense befitting the Godhead, perceive a transmission of will, like the reflexion of an object in a mirror, passing without note of time from Father to Son.[[134]](#cite_note-despiritu-136)

According to [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") the Son prayed to the Father, became a minor to the angels, became incarnate, obeyed the Father as to his human nature; as to his divine nature the Son remained God: "Thus, then, the fact that the Father glorifies, raises up, and exalts the Son does not show that the Son is less than the Father, except in His human nature. For, in the divine nature by which He is equal to the Father, the power of the Father and the Son is the same and their operation is the same."[[73]](#cite_note-dhspriory.org-74) Aquinas stated that the mystery of the Son cannot be explicitly believed to be true without faith in the Trinity (*ST* IIa IIae, 2.7 resp. and 8 resp.).[[135]](#cite_note-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Hierarch_panagia_episcopi_cropped.jpg/250px-Hierarch_panagia_episcopi_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hierarch_panagia_episcopi_cropped.jpg)

A Greek [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") of Athanasius of Alexandria, the chief architect of the Nicene Creed, formulated at Nicaea

[Athanasius of Alexandria](/wiki/Athanasius_of_Alexandria "Athanasius of Alexandria") explained that the Son is eternally one in being with the Father, temporally and voluntarily subordinate in his incarnate ministry.[[136]](#cite_note-athanasius3-138) Such human traits, he argued, were not to be read back into the eternal Trinity. Likewise, the [Cappadocian Fathers](/wiki/Cappadocian_Fathers "Cappadocian Fathers") also insisted there was no economic inequality present within the Trinity. As Basil wrote: "We perceive the operation of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit to be one and the same, in no respect showing differences or variation; from this identity of operation we necessarily infer the unity of nature."[[137]](#cite_note-basil-139)

The traditional theory of "appropriation" consists in attributing certain names, qualities, or operations to one of the Persons of the Trinity, not, however, to the exclusion of the others, but in preference to the others. This theory was established by the Latin Fathers of the fourth and fifth centuries, especially by [Hilary of Poitiers](/wiki/Hilary_of_Poitiers "Hilary of Poitiers"), [Augustine](/wiki/Augustine "Augustine"), and [Leo the Great](/wiki/Leo_the_Great "Leo the Great"). In the Middle Ages, the theory was systematically taught by the [Schoolmen](/wiki/Schoolmen "Schoolmen") such as [Bonaventure](/wiki/Bonaventure "Bonaventure").[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESauvage1907-140)

### Love

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Love")]

Augustine "coupled the doctrine of the Trinity with [anthropology](/wiki/Christian_anthropology "Christian anthropology"). Proceeding from the idea that humans are created by God according to the divine image, he attempted to explain the mystery of the Trinity by uncovering traces of the Trinity in the human personality".[[139]](#cite_note-141) The first key of his exegesis is an interpersonal analogy of mutual love. In *[De trinitate](/wiki/De_trinitate "De trinitate")* (399–419) he wrote:

> We are now eager to see whether that most excellent love is proper to the Holy Spirit, and if it is not so, whether the Father, or the Son, or the Holy Trinity itself is love, since we cannot contradict the most certain faith and the most weighty authority of Scripture which says: "God is love".[[c]](#cite_note-Augustine1-142)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAugustine_of_Hippo200225-143)

One must, therefore, ask if love itself is triune. Augustine found that it is, and consists of "three: the lover, the beloved, and the love".[[d]](#cite_note-Augustine2-144)[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAugustine_of_Hippo200226-145)

Reaffirming the [theopaschite formula](/wiki/Theopaschite_formula "Theopaschite formula") *unus de trinitate passus est carne* (meaning "One of the Trinity suffered in the flesh"),[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPool2011398-146) Thomas Aquinas wrote that Jesus suffered and died as to his human nature, as to his divine nature he could not suffer or die. "But the commandment to suffer clearly pertains to the Son only in His human nature. ... And the way in which Christ was raised up is like the way He suffered and died, that is, in the flesh. For it says in 1 Peter (4:1): 'Christ having suffered in the flesh' ... then, the fact that the Father glorifies, raises up, and exalts the Son does not show that the Son is less than the Father, except in His human nature. For, in the divine nature by which He is equal to the Father."[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAquinas197591-147)

In the 1900s the recovery of a substantially different formula of [theopaschism](/wiki/Theopaschism "Theopaschism") took place: at least *unus de Trinitate passus est* (meaning "not only in the flesh").[[144]](#cite_note-148) More specifically, [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") had an impact not only on the [theodicy](/wiki/Theodicy "Theodicy") of [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") with the [Holocaust theology](/wiki/Holocaust_theology "Holocaust theology"), but also on that of Christianity with a profound rethinking of its [dogmatic theology](/wiki/Dogmatic_theology "Dogmatic theology"). Deeply affected by the [atomic bomb event](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki"),[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYewangoe1987273-149) as early as 1946 the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") theologian [Kazoh Kitamori](/wiki/Kazoh_Kitamori "Kazoh Kitamori") published *Theology of the Pain of God*,[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitamori2005v-150) a [theology of the Cross](/wiki/Theology_of_the_Cross "Theology of the Cross") pushed up to the immanent Trinity. This concept was later taken by both [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches") and [Catholic theology](/wiki/Catholic_theology "Catholic theology"): in 1971 by [Jürgen Moltmann](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Moltmann "Jürgen Moltmann")'s *The Crucified God*; in the 1972 "Preface to the Second Edition" of his 1969 [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") book *Theologie der drei Tage* (English translation: *[The Mystery of Easter](/wiki/Mysterium_Paschale "Mysterium Paschale")*) by [Hans Urs von Balthasar](/wiki/Hans_Urs_von_Balthasar "Hans Urs von Balthasar"), who took a cue from [Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation") 13:8 ([Vulgate](/wiki/Vulgate "Vulgate"): *agni qui occisus est ab origine mundi*, [NIV](/wiki/NIV "NIV"): "the Lamb who was slain from the creation of the world") to explore the "God is love" idea as an "[eternal super-kenosis](/wiki/Eternal_super-kenosis "Eternal super-kenosis")".[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Balthasar2000vii-151) In the words of von Balthasar: "At this point, where the subject undergoing the 'hour' is the Son speaking with the Father, the controversial 'Theopaschist formula' has its proper place: 'One of the Trinity has suffered.' The formula can already be found in [Gregory Nazianzen](/wiki/Gregory_Nazianzen "Gregory Nazianzen"): 'We needed a ... crucified God'."[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvon_Balthasar199255-152) But if theopaschism indicates only a Christological kenosis (or kenotic Christology), instead von Balthasar supports a Trinitarian kenosis:[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMobley2021202-153) "The persons of the Trinity constitute themselves as who they are through the very act of pouring themselves out for each other".[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDimech2019103-154) This allows to clearly distinguish his idea from [Subordinationism](/wiki/Subordinationism "Subordinationism").

Furthermore, following the concepts developed by [Scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism"), the underlying question is whether the three Persons of the Trinity can experience [self-love](/wiki/Self-love "Self-love") (*amor sui*), as well as whether for them, with the conciliar dogmatic formulation in terms that today we would call [ontotheological](/wiki/Ontotheological "Ontotheological"), it is possible for [aseity](/wiki/Aseity "Aseity") (*[causa sui](/wiki/Causa_sui "Causa sui")*) to be valid. If the Father is not the Son or the Spirit since the generator/begetter is not the generated/begotten nor the generation/generative process and vice versa, and since the lover is neither the beloved nor the love dynamic between them and vice versa. As a response, Christianity has provided an [oblative](/wiki/Oblation "Oblation"), sacrificial, martyrizing, crucifying, and precisely kenotic concept of divine ontology.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarson2000chpt._9-155)[[152]](#cite_note-156)

### One God in three persons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: One God in three persons")]

In Trinitarian doctrine, God exists as three persons but is one being, having a single divine [nature](/wiki/Physis_(Christian_theology) "Physis (Christian theology)").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrudem1994226-157) The members of the Trinity are co-equal and co-eternal, one in essence, nature, power, action, and will. As stated in the [Athanasian Creed](/wiki/Athanasian_Creed "Athanasian Creed"), the Father is uncreated, the Son is uncreated, and the Holy Spirit is uncreated, and all three are eternal without beginning.[[154]](#cite_note-athanasian-creed-158) "The Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit" are not names for different parts of God, but one name for God[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarth1975348–349-159) because three persons exist in God as one entity.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPegis1997307–309-160) They cannot be separate from one another. Each person is understood as having the identical essence or nature, not merely similar natures.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_Smet2010-161)

According to the [Eleventh Council of Toledo](/wiki/Eleventh_Council_of_Toledo "Eleventh Council of Toledo") (675) "For, when we say: He who is the Father is not the Son, we refer to the distinction of persons; but when we say: the Father is that which the Son is, the Son that which the Father is, and the Holy Spirit that which the Father is and the Son is, this clearly refers to the nature or substance".[[158]](#cite_note-162)

The [Fourth Lateran Council](/wiki/Fourth_Lateran_Council "Fourth Lateran Council") (1215) adds: "Therefore in God there is only a Trinity, not a quaternity, since each of the three persons is that reality—that is to say substance, essence or divine nature-which alone is the principle of all things, besides which no other principle can be found. This reality neither begets nor is begotten nor proceeds; the Father begets, the Son is begotten and the holy Spirit proceeds. Thus there is a distinction of persons but a unity of nature. Although therefore the Father is one person, the Son another person and the holy Spirit another person, they are not different realities, but rather that which is the Father is the Son and the holy Spirit, altogether the same; thus according to the orthodox and catholic faith they are believed to be consubstantial. "[[159]](#cite_note-163)[[160]](#cite_note-164)

Clarification of the relationships among the three Trinitarian Persons (divine persons, different from the sense of a "human self") advanced in the Magisterial statement promulgated by the [Council of Florence](/wiki/Council_of_Florence "Council of Florence") (1431–1449), though its formulation precedes the council: "These three persons are one God and not three gods, for the three are one substance, one essence, one nature, one Godhead, one infinity, one eternity, and everything (in them) is one
where there is no opposition of relationship [*relationis oppositio*]".[[161]](#cite_note-165)

### *Perichoresis*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Perichoresis")]

Main article: [Perichoresis](/wiki/Perichoresis "Perichoresis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/THE_FIRST_COUNCIL_OF_NICEA.jpg/250px-THE_FIRST_COUNCIL_OF_NICEA.jpg)](/wiki/File:THE_FIRST_COUNCIL_OF_NICEA.jpg)

A depiction of the [Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") in AD 325, at which the Deity of Christ was declared orthodox and [Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") condemned

*Perichoresis* (from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), 'going around', 'envelopment') is a term used by some scholars to describe the relationship among the members of the Trinity. The Latin equivalent for this term is *circumincessio*. This concept refers for its basis to John 10:38,14:11,14:20,[[162]](#cite_note-166) where Jesus is instructing the disciples concerning the meaning of his departure. His going to the Father, he says, is for their sake; so that he might come to them when the "other comforter" is given to them. Then, he says, his disciples will dwell in him, as he dwells in the Father, and the Father dwells in him, and the Father will dwell in them. This is so, according to the theory of *perichoresis*, because the persons of the Trinity "reciprocally contain one another, so that one permanently envelopes and is permanently enveloped by, the other whom he yet envelopes" ([Hilary of Poitiers](/wiki/Hilary_of_Poitiers "Hilary of Poitiers"), *Concerning the Trinity* 3:1).[[163]](#cite_note-hilary-john-167) The most prominent exponent of *perichoresis* was [John of Damascus](/wiki/John_of_Damascus "John of Damascus") (d. 749) who employed the concept as a technical term to describe both the interpenetration of the divine and human natures of Christ and the relationship between the hypostases of the Trinity.[[164]](#cite_note-168)

*Perichoresis* effectively excludes the idea that God has parts, but rather is a [simple being](/wiki/Divine_simplicity "Divine simplicity"). It also harmonizes well with the doctrine that the Christian's union with the Son in his humanity brings him into union with one who contains in himself, in Paul's words, "all the fullness of deity" and not a part.[[e]](#cite_note-169) *Perichoresis* provides an intuitive figure of what this might mean. The Son, the eternal Word, is from all eternity the dwelling place of God; he is the "Father's house", just as the Son dwells in the Father and the Spirit; so that, when the Spirit is "given", then it happens as Jesus said, "I will not leave you as orphans; for I will come to you."[[165]](#cite_note-170)

### Relationship between the persons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Relationship between the persons")]

Although all Trinitarians agree that there exists one God in three persons, Trinitarian theologians have differed on how to explain the relationships of the persons of the Trinity, among them are the [eternal generation of the Son](/wiki/Eternal_generation_of_the_Son "Eternal generation of the Son"),[[166]](#cite_note-:0-171) [the functional subordination of the Son](/wiki/Eternal_functional_subordination "Eternal functional subordination"),[[167]](#cite_note-172) the [eternal procession of the Spirit](/wiki/Eternal_procession_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Eternal procession of the Holy Spirit"),[[168]](#cite_note-173)[[169]](#cite_note-:2-174) the [Filioque](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque")[[170]](#cite_note-175) and the [subordinationism](/wiki/Subordinationism "Subordinationism").[[171]](#cite_note-176)[[172]](#cite_note-177)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/The_church_of_SS_Peter_and_Paul_in_Brockdish_-_stained_glass_-_The_Holy_Trinity.png/250px-The_church_of_SS_Peter_and_Paul_in_Brockdish_-_stained_glass_-_The_Holy_Trinity.png)](/wiki/File:The_church_of_SS_Peter_and_Paul_in_Brockdish_-_stained_glass_-_The_Holy_Trinity.png)

The Holy Trinity on the stained glass windows of the Church of Saint Peter and Saint Paul in [Brockdish](/wiki/Brockdish "Brockdish")

The doctrine of eternal generation is defined as a necessary and eternal act of [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), in which he generates (or begets) [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son") by communicating the whole divine essence to the Son. Generation is not defined as an act of the will, but is by necessity of nature.[[173]](#cite_note-178)[[174]](#cite_note-179) This doctrine has been affirmed by the [Athanasian creed](/wiki/Athanasian_creed "Athanasian creed"),[[175]](#cite_note-180) the [Nicene creed](/wiki/Nicene_creed "Nicene creed")[[176]](#cite_note-181) and by church fathers such as [Athanasius of Alexandria](/wiki/Athanasius_of_Alexandria "Athanasius of Alexandria"), [Augustine](/wiki/Augustine "Augustine"), and [Basil of Caesarea](/wiki/Basil_of_Caesarea "Basil of Caesarea")[[177]](#cite_note-182)[[178]](#cite_note-183)[[179]](#cite_note-184)[[180]](#cite_note-185) being mentioned explicitly first by [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen") of Alexandria.[[181]](#cite_note-186) Those who teach the traditional doctrine of eternal generation have often used biblical texts such as Proverbs 8:23,[[182]](#cite_note-187) Psalm 2:7, Micah 5:2, John 5:26, John 1:18, 3:16, Colossians 1:15, 2 Corinthians 4:4 and Hebrews 1:3 to establish their understanding of eternal generation.[[183]](#cite_note-188)[[184]](#cite_note-:22-189) However, some modern theologians reject the doctrines of eternal generation and procession, disputing the idea that these texts teach the doctrine of eternal generation. To reject eternal generation, [William Lane Craig](/wiki/William_Lane_Craig "William Lane Craig") has argued, is to introduce subordinationism into the Trinity.[[185]](#cite_note-190)[[166]](#cite_note-:0-171)

Among modern Trinitarian debates, the issue of [social Trinitarianism](/wiki/Social_trinitarianism "Social trinitarianism") is often discussed.[[186]](#cite_note-191) Although it is a diverse theological movement, many of its advocates argue that each person of the Trinity has their own center of consciousness and own will united in a loving relationship.[[187]](#cite_note-192) Critics argue it risks veering into tritheism (belief in three gods) by overemphasizing the distinctness of the persons, while proponents say it better reflects the biblical portrayal of the Trinity as relational and active in history. Social Trinitarianism is in contrast to what is often called "[classical Trinitarianism](/wiki/Classical_trinitarianism "Classical trinitarianism")" due to its association with many classical theologians such as Augustine, which instead distinguishes the persons by their eternal relations of begetting and procession.[[188]](#cite_note-193)[[189]](#cite_note-194)

### Nontrinitarianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Nontrinitarianism")]

Main article: [Nontrinitarianism](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism")

[Nontrinitarianism](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism") (or antitrinitarianism) refers to Christian belief systems that reject the doctrine of the Trinity as found in the Nicene Creed as not having a scriptural origin. Nontrinitarian views differ widely on the nature of God, Jesus, and the Holy Spirit.

Various nontrinitarian views, such as [Adoptionism](/wiki/Adoptionism "Adoptionism") and [Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism"), existed prior to the formal definition of the Trinity doctrine in AD 325, 360, and 431 at the Councils of [Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea"), [Constantinople](/wiki/Council_of_Constantinople_(360) "Council of Constantinople (360)"), and [Ephesus](/wiki/Council_of_Ephesus "Council of Ephesus"), respectively.[[190]](#cite_note-vonharnack-195) Adoptionists believed that Jesus became divine only at his baptism, resurrection, or ascension.[[191]](#cite_note-196) Adherents of Arianism postulated that only God has independent existence. Since the Son is dependent, he should, therefore, be called a creature.[[192]](#cite_note-197)

Arianism was condemned as [heretical](/wiki/Heresy#Christianity "Heresy") by the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea#Arian_controversy#Result_of_the_debate "First Council of Nicaea") and, lastly, with [Sabellianism](/wiki/Sabellianism "Sabellianism") by the [Second Ecumenical Council](/wiki/Second_Ecumenical_Council "Second Ecumenical Council").[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlson1999173-198) Adoptionism was declared as heretical by the Ecumenical Council of Frankfurt, convened by the Emperor Charlemagne in 794 for the Latin West Church.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeens201664-199) Following the adoption of trinitarianism at [Constantinople in 381](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople"), [Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") was driven from the Empire, retaining a foothold amongst the Germanic tribes. When the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") converted to Catholicism in 496, however, it gradually faded out.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone2005100-107) Nontrinitarianism was later renewed in the [Gnosticism](/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism") of the [Cathars](/wiki/Cathars "Cathars") in the 11th through 13th centuries, in the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") of the 18th century, and in some groups arising during the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") of the 19th century.[[f]](#cite_note-200)

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Judaism")]

While [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") traditionally rejects the doctrine of the Trinity, some Jewish mystical texts have expressed ideas that bear a resemblance to trinitarian concepts. For example, the *[Zohar](/wiki/Zohar "Zohar")* (AD 1286), a foundational work of [Jewish mysticism](/wiki/Jewish_mysticism "Jewish mysticism"), states that "God is they, and they are it."[[195]](#cite_note-:1-201) This passage has been interpreted by some as referencing a kind of "kabbalistic trinity," describing "three hidden lights" within "the root of all roots"—a unified essence and origin. The parallels between these mystical notions and Christian trinitarianism were striking enough that some medieval Jewish thinkers suggested the Christian Trinity may have arisen from a misinterpretation of Kabbalistic teachings. However, some recent Jewish scholars instead view the *Zohar* as being influenced by Christian trinitarianism. Nevertheless, some later Jewish Aristotelians borrowed from the trinitarian analogies of [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo"), making the claim that God is a thinker, thinking, and thought itself.[[195]](#cite_note-:1-201)

According to [Philo](/wiki/Philo "Philo") (20 BC – c. AD 50), the *Logos*—or divine reason—was the instrument through which God created the world. For Philo, the ultimate Being possesses two primary attributes: goodness and authority. The *Logos* represents the union of these two powers. As pure being, this ultimate source is called the Father; in relation to goodness, he is called God; and in his rule over creation, he is called Lord. The *Logos* is sometimes portrayed not only as the combination of goodness and authority within the Father but also as existing above and between them, thereby being identified with the Supreme Being itself. In this way, Philo presents a kind of trinitarian view of the divine, though it differs from the Christian concept of the Trinity. Scholars continue to debate whether Philo viewed the *Logos* as a distinct person or as an impersonal force.[[196]](#cite_note-202)

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/ClevelandCathedral.jpg/250px-ClevelandCathedral.jpg)](/wiki/File:ClevelandCathedral.jpg)

Three doors representing the trinity at [a cathedral in Cleveland, Ohio](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._John_the_Evangelist_(Cleveland,_Ohio) "Cathedral of St. John the Evangelist (Cleveland, Ohio)")[[197]](#cite_note-203)

Almost every detail of [Gothic cathedrals](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture")—the cruciform plan, the use of light, the statuary, the representation of the Trinity in [trefoils](/wiki/Trefoil "Trefoil") and of the [four evangelists](/wiki/Four_Evangelists "Four Evangelists") in [quatrefoils](/wiki/Quatrefoil "Quatrefoil"), and so on—reveals design decisions that are explicitly theological. However elaborate or simple the architecture, whether purpose-built for Christian use or not, the spaces used for Christian worship speak of theological meanings and values.[[198]](#cite_note-204)

Many Christian churches have three doors symbolizing the Trinity. Other architectural features, such as windows or steps, are also grouped into three for this reason.[[199]](#cite_note-1919Kennedy-205) This practice originated in [spolia](/wiki/Spolia "Spolia") churches that were built from, and on top of, the remains of ancient pre-Christian holy structures.[[200]](#cite_note-206)

Examples are the three [royal doors](/wiki/Royal_doors "Royal doors") inside Eastern churches and the trio of doors in the [façade](/wiki/Fa%C3%A7ade "Façade") of many [cathedrals](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral"). A triangular floor plan can also symbolize the Trinity, as in [Heiligen-Geist-Kapelle](/wiki/Heiligen-Geist-Kapelle,_Bruck "Heiligen-Geist-Kapelle, Bruck") in Austria.[[201]](#cite_note-Bruck-207)

## Artistic depictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Artistic depictions")]

Main article: [The Trinity in art](/wiki/The_Trinity_in_art "The Trinity in art")

The Trinity is most commonly seen in [Christian art](/wiki/Christian_art "Christian art") with the Spirit represented by a dove, as specified in the gospel accounts of the [baptism of Christ](/wiki/Baptism_of_Christ "Baptism of Christ"); he is nearly always shown with wings outspread. However, depictions using three human figures appear occasionally in most periods of art.[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchiller1971figs_1;_5–16-208)

The Father and the Son are usually differentiated by age and later by dress, but this too is not always the case. The usual depiction of the Father as an older man with a white beard may derive from the biblical [Ancient of Days](/wiki/Ancient_of_Days "Ancient of Days"), which is often cited in defense of this sometimes controversial representation. However, in [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), the Ancient of Days is usually understood to be God the Son, not God the Father (see below)—early [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") images show Christ as the Ancient of Days,[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECartlidgeElliott2001240-209) but this [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") became rare. When the Father is depicted in art, he is sometimes shown with a [halo](/wiki/Halo_(religious_iconography) "Halo (religious iconography)") shaped like an [equilateral triangle](/wiki/Equilateral_triangle "Equilateral triangle") instead of a circle. The Son is often shown at the Father's right hand (Acts 7:56). He may be represented by a symbol—typically the Lamb (*[agnus dei](/wiki/Agnus_dei "Agnus dei")*) or a cross—or on a [crucifix](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix"), so that the Father is the only human figure shown at full size. In early medieval art, the Father may be represented by a hand appearing from a cloud in a blessing gesture, for example, in scenes of the [Baptism of Christ](/wiki/Baptism_of_Christ "Baptism of Christ"). Later, in the West, the Throne of Mercy (or "Throne of Grace") became a common depiction. In this style, the Father (sometimes seated on a [throne](/wiki/Throne "Throne")) is shown supporting either a [crucifix](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix")[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchiller1971122–124_and_figs_409–414-210) or, later, a slumped crucified Son, similar to the [Pietà](/wiki/Piet%C3%A0 "Pietà") (this type is distinguished in German as the *Not Gottes*)[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchiller1971219–224_and_figs_768–804-211) in his outstretched arms, while the Dove hovers above or in between them. This subject continued to be popular until at least the 18th century.

By the end of the 15th century, larger representations, other than the Throne of Mercy, became effectively standardized, showing an older figure in plain robes for the Father, Christ with his torso partly bare to display the wounds of his [Passion](/wiki/Passion_(Christianity) "Passion (Christianity)"), and the dove above or around them. In earlier representations, both Father, especially, and Son often wear elaborate robes and crowns. Sometimes the Father alone wears a crown or even a [papal tiara](/wiki/Papal_tiara "Papal tiara").

In the later part of the [Christian Era](/wiki/Christian_Era "Christian Era"), in Renaissance European iconography, the [Eye of Providence](/wiki/Eye_of_Providence "Eye of Providence") began to be used as an explicit image of the Christian Trinity and associated with the concept of [Divine Providence](/wiki/Divine_Providence "Divine Providence"). Seventeenth-century depictions of the Eye of Providence sometimes show it surrounded by clouds or [sunbursts](/wiki/Sunburst "Sunburst").[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPotts198268–78-212)
